canada.interpretation: Current Canadian state: Consumer affordability stress. Canadian energy affordability pressure is active: Canadian-dollar oil and consumer energy costs are elevated while rates, currency movements, and debt service limit household room. Resource-producing-region expansion is active: Resource GDP, Alberta crude production, exports, and national crude production are rising, indicating expansion in producing-region activity.

Exports or pipeline timing
Seasonal inventory movement

limitations: Broader inflation
Workforce-composition effects
Wage measurement effects

limitations: Cold weather

affordability.interpretation: Household affordability evidence remains mixed or context-dependent
affordability.scope: Canadian household affordability with global commodity context

mixed.label: Canada food-price-to-income index
mixed.reason: The indicator provides partial or ambiguous evidence. A rising affordability ratio or growth gap means the documented cost is increasing relative to income or wages.
mixed.group: Food
mixed.calculation: Rebased quarterly food CPI / rebased disposable income per person × 100
limitations: Derived ratio. Normalized to 100 at 2017-01-01. Only completed quarters with all three monthly observations are included. Statistics Canada table 18-10-0004-01 may revise recent and historical observations; classifications and baskets follow the native table. Normalized to 100 at 2017-01-01. Income is a seasonally adjusted annual rate in millions of CAD and is not divided by four; population is the matching quarterly estimate. Statistics Canada table 36-10-0112-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations. Values are published at seasonally adjusted annual rates. Statistics Canada table 17-10-0009-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations.
Country baskets, weights, index bases, seasonal treatment, and housing institutions differ. Compare growth rates and within-country percentiles before levels.

mixed.label: Canada food wage-pressure gap
mixed.reason: The indicator provides partial or ambiguous evidence. A rising affordability ratio or growth gap means the documented cost is increasing relative to income or wages.
mixed.group: Food
mixed.calculation: Food CPI YoY - average hourly wage YoY
limitations: Derived difference. Statistics Canada table 18-10-0004-01 may revise recent and historical observations; classifications and baskets follow the native table. Statistics Canada table 14-10-0063-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations.
Country baskets, weights, index bases, seasonal treatment, and housing institutions differ. Compare growth rates and within-country percentiles before levels.

Statistics Canada table 36-10-0112-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations. Values are published at seasonally adjusted annual rates. Statistics Canada table 17-10-0009-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations.

food.interpretation: Food affordability evidence is mixed across costs and purchasing power

supporting.label: Canada real wage growth
supporting.reason: The indicator is consistent with this interpretation. Rising real income or wages generally increase purchasing power, while household experiences can differ from the average.

mixed.label: Canada grocery wage-pressure gap
mixed.reason: The indicator provides partial or ambiguous evidence. A rising affordability ratio or growth gap means the documented cost is increasing relative to income or wages.
mixed.group: Food

limitations: Derived difference. Statistics Canada table 18-10-0004-01 may revise recent and historical observations; classifications and baskets follow the native table. Statistics Canada table 14-10-0063-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations.

mixed.label: Household saving rate
mixed.reason: The indicator provides partial or ambiguous evidence. The saving rate can fall because households are confident or because essential costs are absorbing income; supporting evidence is required.
mixed.group: Purchasing power
mixed.calculation: Official quarterly household saving as a percentage of household disposable income.
limitations: Statistics Canada table 36-10-0112-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations. Values are published at seasonally adjusted annual rates.
Country baskets, weights, index bases, seasonal treatment, and housing institutions differ. Compare growth rates and within-country percentiles before levels.

contradicting.label: Canada real household disposable income per person
contradicting.reason: The indicator moves against this interpretation. Rising real income or wages generally increase purchasing power, while household experiences can differ from the average.
contradicting.calculation: Nominal disposable income per person / quarterly-average all-items CPI × 100
limitations: Deflated by the aligned all-items CPI; this is a derived CPI-deflated measure, not an official chained-dollar series. Income is a seasonally adjusted annual rate in millions of CAD and is not divided by four; population is the matching quarterly estimate. Statistics Canada table 36-10-0112-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations. Values are published at seasonally adjusted annual rates. Statistics Canada table 17-10-0009-01 is latest-vintage and may revise recent or historical observations. Only completed quarters with all three monthly observations are included. Statistics Canada table 18-10-0004-01 may revise recent and historical observations; classifications and baskets follow the native table.
Country baskets, weights, index bases, seasonal treatment, and housing institutions differ. Compare growth rates and within-country percentiles before levels.
